data.title: Market Outlook For 2026: The Fourth Industrial Revolution Is Just Getting Started
data.description:
As part of my due diligence for securities that I am interested in, I follow the macro events that dictate which investments may perform better at various times throughout the economic cycle. Despite all the recent noise about the economy in the U.S., and geopolitical rumblings heard around the world, I am optimistic regarding the stock market in 2026. After all, we are only about 3 years into the latest bull market and tailwinds from the Fourth Industrial Revolution, or 4IR (also called Industry 4.0) are just beginning to propel the S&P 500 (SP500) to new highs in 2025 which I believe will continue for at least another year or two.

The term Fourth Industrial Revolution was coined by Klaus Schwab, Founder and Executive Chairman of the World Economic Forum. He introduced this concept in his book, The Fourth Industrial Revolution, published in 2016. In it, he discusses how emerging technologies like artificial intelligence the Internet of Things, and robotics have begun to merge with the physical, digital and biological worlds and, thus, have revolutionized economies, industries and societies in the process.

History of US Bull and Bear Markets

This chart from First Trust illustrates the historical cycle of bull and bear markets since 1942. The average bull market has lasted 4.3 years while the average bear market lasted just under 1 year.

Yogi Berra once said, “it’s tough to make predictions, especially about the future.” Well life is tough and investors want to know what to expect, so here it is. My prediction is that the S&P 500 will end 2026 at a level about 10% higher than the end of 2025. The current S&P 500 index is right about 6,850 today so I will use that number as the baseline for my prediction, putting my expected ending S&P 500 value as of 12/31/26 at 7,240.

I believe that the drivers for the current bull market are still in place to drive further growth, albeit slower growth than we have experienced over the past 3 years. The current bull market that started in early 2023 still has another year or two left in it in my opinion. The prospect of likely further interest rate cuts should provide some stimulus for that growth as well.

I believe that the growth will slow slightly from 2025 levels in 2026 but the momentum from companies benefiting from the buildout of AI data centers and all the energy and power infrastructure necessary to support all that expansion will continue to drive further growth next year.

The drivers for that mid-range growth of about 10% next year (slower than the 20%+ in 2023 and 2024 and the 16.5% YTD growth) include many of the positive impacts that we are seeing on companies that are benefitting from advances in AI, robotics, virtual reality, quantum computing, the Internet of Things (connected intelligent devices), blockchain, and other advanced Industry 4.0 technologies.

Businesses are undergoing transformation and those companies that are able to innovate and leverage the use of new or advanced technologies in their business models stand to benefit the most from 4IR. The chart below from Research.com suggests that changes in customer expectations are also impacting the digitalization of business models and product innovation.

Growth Stocks Benefiting from 4IR With Momentum Heading into 2026

I have been writing about 4IR and stocks that have shown great growth potential due to the business benefits from the application of Industry 4.0 technologies since March 2023 when I first covered Powell Industries (POWL), calling it a Growth Stock for the Fourth Industrial Revolution. Since that initial coverage when I rated POWL stock a Hold, it delivered a total return of nearly 650%.

Despite the big runup in price, POWL gets Buy ratings from both Wall Street and SA Analysts and has very good Quant scores and appears to have more upside ahead with a $1.4B backlog. This comment from CEO Metcalf on the POWL Q4 2025 earnings report summarizes the company outlook for 2026 from their perspective:

"We are confident that the strong commercial momentum we experienced across our key end markets in fiscal 2025 will carry into fiscal 2026."

Another example of a company that has realized benefits from its investments in Industry 4.0 technologies that I first covered in July 2023 is Flex (FLEX), formerly Flextronics International. This was what I wrote in the summary of that article:

  • Flex, formerly known as Flextronics International, is embracing AI and machine learning to optimize manufacturing processes, reduce material loss, and improve customer satisfaction.
  • The company is leveraging Industry 4.0 technologies in areas such as automation, connectivity, and continuous improvement in processes.

FLEX currently gets Strong Buy ratings from Wall Street and expects to see 35% data center revenue growth in FY26 as AI-driven demand accelerates.

Yet another example of a company that I have covered multiple times that has seen benefits from 4IR trends and advancements is Sterling Infrastructure (STRL). I wrote about STRL in February 2024 when I rated the stock a Buy and it has delivered a 282% return since then. This was what I wrote in that article:

Like other small-cap growth companies that I covered in my recent article, the Fourth Industrial Revolution, or Industry 4.0 as it is also called, is driving some of the growth opportunities for STRL. Although the company may not directly offer Industry 4.0 technology as part of their business practices, those trends are at least in part responsible for driving business growth.

Another growth stock that I first covered in July 2024 when I rated it my Best Growth Stock for 2024 is Power Solutions International (PSIX). That stock has also experienced significant outperformance in the past two years due to data center demands and a return to profitability as a result. The stock has appreciated more than 450% since that article was published and is up nearly 100% in the past year.

Although the PSIX stock price plummeted after the Q3 earnings report which included 60% EPS growth, it still appears inexpensive and gets Strong Buy ratings from Wall Street. The CEO indicated a very positive outlook for growth continuing to ramp up in his earnings summary (bold emphasis mine).

Dino Xykis, Chief Executive Officer, said, “We achieved the highest sales in our company’s history this quarter, delivering strong financial performance with sales increasing 62% and net income rising 59%. These results underscore the robust demand for our power systems solutions, particularly within the data center market. During the quarter, we expanded our manufacturing capacity and increased production across key data center product lines. We are continuing to ramp up production to ensure on-time delivery while implementing targeted operational improvements to enhance efficiency, execution and future growth going forward.”

There are other examples of growth stocks that I have not previously covered in detail but that I have been following and that are also seeing benefits from the AI data center buildout and growing demands for the infrastructure to support the power and energy needs of this blossoming industry. A few examples that come to mind include:

Argan, Inc. (AGX), Vertiv Holdings (VRT), Dycom Industries (DY) and Primoris Services (PRIM). The chart below shows the 3-year total return for each of those 4 stocks compared to the S&P 500.

Blockchain Technology and Bitcoin

According to this article on 4IR from Research.com, Klaus Schwab described blockchain as the heart of the Fourth Industrial Revolution.

One of the key features of blockchain technology is that it makes an irreversible timeline of data, as each block is always stored chronologically. Transactions remain visible to all users in the network, and these cannot be changed or altered once added to the shared ledger. This linear, linked structure prevents users from making changes to transactions, making the system tamper-proof. One application of blockchain currently gaining traction is Bitcoin. Experts have explored the potential of Bitcoin and blockchain technology in the shift to a new economic system under the Fourth Industrial Revolution. According to Blockchain co-founder Nicolas Cary, Bitcoin and blockchain can prove crucial to the shift to the circular economy, due to Bitcoin’s ability to perform frictionless, transparent financial transactions without the need for intermediaries.

Whether or not you understand the “value” in bitcoin (BTC-USD), it is here to stay and has been growing in both price and demand over time from financial institutions and intermediaries all over the world. As an investment, Bitcoin has far outpaced the S&P 500 over the past 3 years despite its recent pullback.

In fact, Blackrock recently announced that its Bitcoin holdings have risen more than 250% since the SEC approval of spot Bitcoin ETFs in Q4 2024.

Beyond cryptocurrencies blockchain technology has other important uses as well that are being driven by Industry 4.0 demands including the use of “smart contracts”.

To have the capability to validate, verify, capture, and implement agreements among parties, smart contracts combine with Distributed Ledger Technology and the Industrial Internet of Things. After collecting actual, legal events, a smart contract gathers IoT data—business processes, meters, sensors—to allow for performance assessment. Afterward, this information communicates the automated rules of a contract by posting outcomes and supplemental evidence to the blocks (Schmitt et al., 2019).

Risks and Challenges to 4IR

Beyond the ethical concerns around AI, there are additional potential challenges associated with the Fourth Industrial Revolution that could derail the growth story in the coming years. The rapid pace of technological innovation requires governments to become more “agile”. Governments, including the U.S. and other developed nations in particular, must develop sustainable models for adapting to 4IR. In other words, governments and private industry must work together in making policy and developing regulations to support innovation while minimizing societal impacts.

Businesses must also become more agile and adapt to changing customer expectations. Concerns around privacy and transparency around the use of “big data” need to be addressed. For example, Salesforce (CRM) witnessed the impact of souring consumer sentiment in 2025 around its product offerings despite seeing substantial demand for its AI-centric agentic business model. Even after reporting excellent results in its latest quarterly report and after delivering upbeat guidance for 2026, CRM is the 2nd worst performer in the Dow index in 2025.

"CRM reported its FY3Q26 results with the top-line coming in line with Street expectations while seeing a significant bottom-line beat with better-than-expected FY4Q26 top-line guidance as the company continues to invest aggressively in its Agentforce strategy to reaccelerate growth," said analysts led by Dan Ives.

The analysts added that Salesforce provided fourth quarter fiscal 2026 guidance which came in above Street estimates on the top-line while meeting the Street’s EPS estimate as the company balances investments into growth with margin expansion and free cash flow, or FCF, generation.

"This was a step in the right direction for CRM with a long way to go as the Agentforce strategy builds incremental momentum with a strong pipeline to capitalize on while balancing investments into growth initiatives with bottom-line expansion over the coming quarters," said Ives and his team.

Other risks include the massive investment in AI data centers and the infrastructure needed to support the growing power demands. This recent report from S&P Global highlights those risks.

Data center and AI infrastructure investment has grown rapidly, with its contribution to economic growth already evident. Sector market fundamentals are healthy, and digital infrastructure has become a central theme for investors and lenders. With the AI transformation still in its early stages, we expect this will remain the case for several years, supported by robust demand, limited supply, strong earnings, high prices and narrow corporate spreads.

However, with such capital intensity and investor appetite, competition is also increasing fast. This could lead to higher leverage, unsustainable asset valuations, reduced spread differentiation across the asset-quality spectrum and more aggressive financing structures. These are all typical ingredients for busts and highlight the importance of data center risk management.

Other longer-term risks for data centers include overbuilding, lower residual values, obsolescence, tenant credit quality and regulatory requirements. We believe these risks will not be uniform among market participants should AI revenue promises fade.

The associated risk with all this massive capex spending by many of the big tech companies like Nvidia, Amazon, Meta, Google, and others is that it has led to concentration risk in the S&P 500. The top 10 holdings in the SPY account for nearly 40% of the total market value.

However, as we enter the month of December 2025 there is some sector rotation occurring which broadens the depth of the bull market, offering additional growth opportunity in 2026 for many of the companies outside of the top 10. As fellow analyst, Lawrence Fuller writes, this broadening of the bull market is another investment theme to watch in 2026.

This will be one of my investment themes for 2026, as I see the improvement in earnings growth breadth narrowing the sector performance differential that has been the most outstanding characteristic of this bull market.

Additional macro risks to consider that could influence my prediction for a mildly bullish market in 2026 include:

  • Potential for rising inflation as the US economy re-accelerates.
  • De-globalization of supply chains and manufacturing, contributing to a continued debasement of the USD, leading to a shift toward non-US investments and a further surge in gold prices.
  • AI “bubble” bursting, causing the Mag 7 stocks to suffer a major market correction and impacting the broader market.
  • More private credit “cockroaches” appear and the credit markets see wider spreads leading to a contraction in fixed income investments as risk accelerates.

And of course, there is always the possibility of a “Black Swan” event, something that nobody can predict. If that happens, all bets are off. Meanwhile, I recommend staying invested and do not veer from your investment strategy unless you are completely dependent on NVDA stock going to the stratosphere. In that case, you may want to adjust your strategy to account for that concentration risk.

data.title: S&P 500: Junk Bonds Indicate Prolonged Bull Run
data.description:
S&P 500: why junk bonds are a breadth signal

Like many of you, my inbox is flooded by bearish calls on the equity market as all equity indices (including SP500, DJI, NASDAQ, and also RTY) hover near all-time-highs. These bearish calls are supported by various reasons that can cause an “imminent market correction.” These reasons range from old-fashioned high P/E to more exotic ones like the high Buffett Indicator, worsening market liquidity, the excessive concentration on the magnificent 7 stocks, the rule of 20, etc. I’ve published a few articles recently to closely examine these arguments and explain why they can misleading (for example, my latest one is titled “S&P 500: Rule Of 20 Likely To Remain Misleading In 2026”).

After writing several of these, the intellectual stimulation of making counterarguments began to wear off. Deconstructing old things can be fun for a while, but eventually constructing something new is more lasting – and also rewarding - intellectually. As such, in this article, instead keep explaining why bears’ thesis are flawed, I will present an alternative market indicator that I have been using over the years (and with good success fortunately): junk bond yield. The remainder of this article will explain A) why and how I use junk bond yields to assess the breadth of a bull market, and B) why what I believe current junk bond yields point to a prolonged bull run.

With that, let me directly get to the point with the following chart. The chart details Moody’s seasoned BAA corporate bond in the past 20 years. As seen, the current yield is 5.85%, which is slightly above the average yield over this period (indicated by the dashed line at 5.37%), a period long enough to roughly span a complete macro debt cycle in my view. As seen, the yield has reached almost 10% in late 2008 or early 2009, reflecting the financial crisis. This peak was followed by a secular decline and bottomed at around 3% amid the epic easing after the COVID pandemic. The yield has broadly increased since then more or less reverted to the long-term mean.

Such a more-or-less normal yield for BAA bonds sends two signals to me. First, it indicates that the prevailing sentiment toward corporate credit and liquidity is normal (and normal means healthy in my book). Second, it also indicates to me that investors’ optimism has not gone too extreme. I am not too concerned when investors are willing to pay a high premium for assets with higher quality, and I am very concerned when the enthusiasm extends to assets with sub-par quality (such as those with BAA rating or lower).

Junk bond yield

At this point, some careful readers probably are already yelling at me – but Moody's seasoned Baa corporate bonds are not considered junk bonds! Indeed, the definition of junk bond is vague to start with. Bonds with credit ratings below the investment grade threshold are often called junk bonds. Different rating agencies use different terminologies and picture can become confusing. In this article, I define junk bonds as those with credit ratings of BB+ and lower in S&P or Fitch terminologies or Ba1 and lower in Moody's. By this definition, the BAA yields cited above do not represent junk bond yield. But they do represent corporate bonds with weaker credit ratings, and their yields correlate closely with those with even lower ratings as shown in the next picture below.

To wit, the next chart below shows the yield from the Vanguard High-Yield Corporate Fund Investor Shares (VWEHX) in the past 30 years. VWEHX is the oldest high-yield bond mutual fund that I can find with an inception date in 1978 and thus provides plenty of historical data for this analysis. As seen, VWEHX yield has changed in very similar patters as the BAA corporate bond yield shows above, with a peak around 2008~2009, a bottom near 2022~2023, and a current reading very close to the long-term mean (6.15% vs. 6.19%).

And VWEHX’s holdings are well-aligned with my above definition of junk bond. As seen in the second p picture below, VWEHX is heavily exposure to sub-par bonds with ratings below BBB. Its largest holdings are rated BB, accounting for almost ½ (or 49.67% to be exact) of its total assets. The next largest holdings are rated B, accounting for about 1/3 of its total assets.

BAA credit spread

Another reason that I am not alarmed – at least in the near term - is the credit yield spread between BAA and risk-free rates. To wit, the chart below displays the spread between Moody’s Seasoned Baa Corporate Bond Yield Relative to the Yield on 10-Year Treasury Constant Maturity in the past 5 years. This spread represents the extra yield investors demand to hold lower-tier corporate debt compared to the risk-free 10-Year Treasury, thus serving as another indicator for investors risk appetite.

The spread has fluctuated between around 2.4% and 1.4% during this periods. The current 1.76% reading sits slightly below my estimated five-year average of 1.9%, which can signal a certain degree of risk appetite of market participants. However, I believe the appetite is quite moderate and largely reflects the anticipation of the rate cuts based on the latest announcements made by the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC). Based on FOMC’s latest comments and dot-plot, I see good odds for 1 or 2 rate cuts in the next 1~2 years total 25 to 50 basis points. These cuts, if materialized, could potentially widen the credit yield between BAA bonds and 10-year treasury rates to a range of 2.01% and 2.26%, slightly above the 5-year average.

Other risks and final thoughts

Let me close by iterating a key word I used in my above projection – near term. My above outlook is meant for the next 1~2 years based on my judgment of current macroeconomic condition and Fed’s dot-plot. In the longer term, tighter credit spread does show a close correlation with market downturns and vice versa – that is why I monitor junk bond yields and their spread relative to risk-free rates in the first place.

As an example, the last figure shows the long-term relationship between the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) price movement (top panel) and the VWEHX yield spread relative to 10-year treasury rates (the bottom panel). Thanks to both SPY and VWEHX’s long history, we have data to examine their correlation over several market cycles. As seen, even a casual look can reveal an inverse correlation between SPY price movements and the spread between VWEHX and treasury rates. I added some red and green arrows to further highlight several key market junctures. The red arrows show that surges in the spread have often been followed by large SPY price rallies. Vice versa, deep SPY price corrections have been often preceded by below average spread, and the current spread is below the average observed in this long period.

For me, taking an average for this such a long period (of almost 5 decades) can be misleading as I believe future interest rates won’t (or cannot) be as high as those observed before 2000 (as detailed in my latest article on the Rule Of 20).

To close, the goal of this article is to caution readers about the various “market peak indictors” I see more and more often these days in my inbox. These indicators all have limitations and could be misleading under current market conditions. Given what I see, specially in terms of junk bond yields, my verdict is that the risk of a drastic allocation away from equity is far larger than the risks of staying invested under current conditions. In my own accounts and also our Investing Group Service (Envision Early Retirement), I apply a dynamic allocation model to perform gradual and disciplined adjustment of equity-bond ratio based on their relative valuation using metrics discussed in this article. There are of course other alternative/better approaches, but my point is that it is much better to follow a disciplined approach rather than keep guessing the market top.

data.title: Stoke Therapeutics: A Long-Term Biotech Growth Play - Why I Assign A Hold Rating
data.description:
Stoke Therapeutics, Inc. (NASDAQ: STOK) stock is enjoying a great year. The past 6 months saw its stock gaining over 180%. Such gains could be already pricing in Phase 3 success of its lead pipeline. The company isn't just developing a drug but pioneering a new class of medicine. Its platform has the potential to redefine treatment for rare diseases and I think the company stands on the verge of a major breakthrough. I, however, assign a HOLD rating on the stock considering the long way towards pipeline success.

Stoke's Business Model

Stoke is a developer of RNA-based medicines for rare genetic diseases like Dravet syndrome. The company's lead drug candidate, zorevunersen, is currently in Phase 3 trials. The company currently funds its operations through R&D collaborations plus cash reserves. Looking into the future, the company's customers will be patients and payments will be coming from healthcare systems should the drug be approved.

Assessing Pipeline

Stock's pipeline is promising led by its zorevunersen drug. The drug is in its pivotal Phase 3 trial and has shown strong early enrollment. The latest update on the drug is that its four years of data is showing not just reduced seizures but also durable cognitive improvements. The disease-modifying potential the drug carries is a potential blockbuster in an around 38,000 patient market.

Stoke also has early-stage programs for two rare genetic disorders. STK-002 for ADOA, an optic nerve disease, is in Phase 1. Meanwhile, the SYNGAP1 (a neurodevelopmental disorder) program is still in preclinical research. Interestingly, both programs are applying the same clever science of boosting protein expression from the healthy gene copy. I view this strategy as a high-risk but the science is quite compelling.

The TANGO Platform

Stoke has a very unique proprietary technology, Targeted Augmentation of Nuclear Gene Output (TANGO). If approved, zorevunersen's success in Dravet syndrome will be a key validation of the entire TANGO platform.

Stoke carries the potential to transform from a one-asset bet into a credible platform company with a repeatable playbook for treating haploinsufficiency diseases. I believe that the company's long-term value lies in this proven engine for drug creation.

Brief Q3 Update

Stoke reported a good set of Q3 results illustrating a perfect pre-revenue biotech entity executing its strategy well. The company reported a revenue beat but not driven by product sales but rather collaboration funding from Biogen. The company received payment from Biogen for licensing rights and to share development costs for its lead drug outside of North America.

That said, the real story is the company's financial fortitude behind its science. Stoke ended the quarter with a strong $328 million in cash. This was even after a significant operational burn. The company even reported that this cash position provides a runway into mid-2028.

The Biogen Collaboration

Earlier this year, Stoke entered into a biotech licensing deal which saw it selling its commercial rights to its lead drug outside North America to Biogen.

I believe this deal gave the company some massive advantages and that the drug's potential has been maximised.

That said, there is a potential downside. The company could be sacrificing long-term international revenue for near-term financial stability and development support. Stoke's bet is that teaming up with Biogen's global power gives it a better chance of success in North America than trying to go it alone.

In another thinking, this Biogen deal somehow sets the stage for a future buyout. Biogen will be left owning the global rights except for North America, assuming the drug succeeds. This could appear a bit messy for the company and buying the whole company could be a simple fix on Biogen's part. This makes Stoke a potential future acquisition target. I believe that this adds a potential M&A premium to the company's story beyond just the drug's sales potential.

Competition

The company's main competitor isn't just another drug but the entire gene-therapy approach. Its main competitor is Encoded Therapeutics which is also targeting Dravet's root cause. It's doing so through advancing a one-time gene-regulating therapy, ETX101. Encoded has already reported promising early clinical data.

Stoke's moat hinges on its modality. Because it’s an antisense oligonucleotide, zorevunersen can be given repeatedly and adjusted in dose. I believe that this offers a crucial safety advantage when compared with permanent and one-time gene therapy. Should long-term data continue to show not just seizure reduction but durable cognitive gains, I think that reversible benefit could become its unassailable moat. This could make the drug a preferred choice for the lifelong neurological condition.

There is also a massive commercial advantage to Stoke's model that appeals to both investors and payers. Its chronic annuity model is much more manageable than a one-time cure model which brings with it a multi-million-dollar price tag. That could terrify payers. Stoke's model makes it far easier for insurance companies to budget for and to pay. I believe that this makes it a much smoother and more reliable business.

Other Key Risks to Watch

The company is also faced with other real risks beyond that of competition. Its Phase 3 trial could simply fail to hit its endpoints convincingly. Should that happen, it'll be left with great science but with zero marketable product. Also, regulatory pathways are not as predictable. The FDA might wake up demanding more data thereby delaying launch for more years.

The other question relates to commercialisation. The company's ability to then be able to build a sales force and secure reimbursement to reach patients is an area of scrutiny as it can leave the company exposed. The bottom line is that a breakthrough therapy is worthless if it fails to get to the people who need it.

Valuation

The company's forward P/E of 656.77 is 3,287.42% higher than the sector median of 19.39. This valuation is not based on earnings fundamentals because such are non-existent. Its entirely based on speculative pipeline potential. It appears this P/E level versus the sector is showing that the market has already priced in a zorevunersen Phase 3 success. I think this leaves the stock overvalued with no margin for error. Any setback would potentially result in a sharp correction.

Conclusion

I think Stoke's story presents a compelling opportunity. The company's validated platform, a cash runway through key milestones makes the path very clear. This position is also cemented by a solid strategic partner in Biogen. I believe that the potential for both commercial success and a strategic buyout are both high. That said, I still think Stoke is a high-risk, high-reward opportunity. Its pipeline justifies investor optimism but not a new buy at current levels. I assign a HOLD rating on the stock.

data.title: AI Bubble Or Supercycle? AMD CEO Says Quiet Part Out Loud
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Lisa Su recently spoke at UBS’s Global Technology and AI Conference.

The Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) CEO doubled down on the idea that we are not in an AI bubble, but rather “two years into a ten-year super cycle” of compute demand.

Moreover, she laid out why AMD is structurally positioned to capture a double-digit share of a now $1 trillion data center total addressable market, or TAM, by 2030.

The conversation reinforced several pillars of my original AMD thesis.

First, that AMD’s pivot to high-performance computing and AI is deliberate, multi-year, and working.

Secondly, the chiplet-first architecture and rack-scale systems offer a genuine competitive advantage.

And, most importantly, that AI is lifting both accelerators and CPUs, creating a second wave of upside that he market still isn’t fully pricing in.

In short, this UBS appearance made me even more confident that AMD can grow into a trillion-dollar valuation over the next cycle.

I continue to see a path to $775 per share and, in a bull case, something closer to $1,000.

From “CPU Underdog” To Data Centre Powerhouse

The first thing Su hammered home is that AMD is no longer a PC/CPU underdog trying to claw back share. It is now structurally a data center company.

Over the last few years, AMD has deliberately reoriented its R&D and product roadmaps around high-performance computing and AI.

We’ve been incredibly focused on high-performance computing… and then a few years ago, it became absolutely clear that this was going to be all about AI… So yes, we’ve pivoted our R&D capabilities… to a significant focus on high-performance computing and AI.

Source: Lisa Su, UBS Conference.

We can't deny that this pivot has worked so far.

AMD’s data center business has been growing over 50% a year and, according to Su, is set to accelerate from “50% plus… to over 60% plus as we go forward.”

Data center has gone from under 20% of the business to nearly half.

$1T TAM With Double-Digit Share Ambitions

At its Analyst Day, AMD lifted its data center TAM estimate to $1 trillion by 2030, up from $500 billion by 2028. At UBS, Su reiterated not just the TAM, but their share ambition.

You really have to have a holistic view… CPUs, GPUs, FPGAs, the possibility of doing ASICs, it’s being able to integrate all of that together. And that’s our unique capability… we’re the only semiconductor company out there that has all of this foundational IP.

Source: Lisa Su, UBS Conference.

This claim is not unsubstantiated, but rather well grounded in a portfolio that spans CPUs, GPUs and semi-custom / ASIC-like work, all tied together by common IP and a chiplet-based design philosophy.

Chiplets are how you mix and match compute, I/O, and memory without restarting from scratch each cycle, which provides a structural edge in a world where workloads, models and memory requirements are shifting every year.

ASICs vs GPUs: Why Lisa Thinks GPUs Still Win This Cycle

One of the more interesting exchanges was about whether custom ASICs will “eat” the GPU accelerator market.

Su’s view is nuanced, and she gave credit where credit is due.

Google has done a great job with the TPU architecture… But it is a more purpose-built architecture. It’s not built with the same programmability… the same capabilities to do training and inference where GPUs are.

Source: Lisa Su, UBS Conference.

Models and workloads are changing fast, and developers want maximum flexibility. That favors programmable GPUs, not fixed-function ASICs.

Our view is… over the next 5 years or so, we’ll see GPUs still be the significant majority of the market because we are still so early in the cycle and because software developers actually want the flexibility to innovate on different algorithms.

Source: Lisa Su, UBS Conference.

Lisa Su still sees ASICs as 20–25% of the market, which is exactly the framework I’ve been using.

ASICs will grow, but they are a complement, not a death sentence for GPUs. For AMD, that’s extremely important. It means the sweet spot of the market, general-purpose, high-performance accelerators, remains attractive and large enough for a second major vendor.

From Silicon To Systems: Helios, ZT and Rack-Scale AI

Another key theme from the UBS talk is AMD’s ongoing transition from being “just a chip company” to being a systems company, something I highlighted after the Q3 print with the Helios platform.

Su was very explicit about why AMD is doing this:

The reason we’re doing this integration is the time to useful capability… is super critical. So the full-stack solution is a way for us to help customers get to productive compute capacity.

Source: Lisa Su, UBS Conference.

The ZT Systems acquisition and Sanmina partnership give AMD the ability to offer rack-level, reference designs like Helios, which integrate MI400/MI450 GPUs, EPYC CPUs and high-speed networking into an open, Meta-co-designed rack architecture.

Best-in-class system design… combined with our best-in-class hardware and software capability… We’re super excited about MI450 and the Helios product that will come to market in 2026.

Source: Lisa Su, UBS Conference.

This is exactly how Nvidia (NVDA) built its moat. They don’t just sell chips; the offer a whole ecosystem.

AMD is now following the same playbook, but with an open rack ecosystem that hyperscalers and cloud providers may eventually prefer.

AI Is Not A Bubble, It’s A 10-Year Super Cycle

The other big takeaway was Su’s stance on the “AI bubble” debate. She was blunt:

There’s not a concept of a bubble. What there is a concept of is, we are, let’s call it, 2 years into a 10-year super cycle.

Source: Lisa Su, UBS Conference.

From her vantage point, talking to “the largest AI users out there”, the constraint isn’t optimism, it’s compute:

The one thing that is constant as we talk to customers is we need more compute… if there was more compute installed… we would get to the answer faster.

Source: Lisa Su, UBS Conference.

On ROI, she pointed to AMD’s own internal experience over the past 15–18 months:

What started as ‘let’s try AI for our internal use cases’ has now turned into significant clear productivity wins… there is no question that there is a return on investment for investment in AI.

Source: Lisa Su, UBS Conference.

AI capex is not pure speculation; it is becoming embedded in productivity, product development and service delivery. The gains are definitely there, even if not for everyone, at least the big tech companies seem to be benefiting from an AI productivity boost.

CPUs, PCs and AI Agents

One of the more under-appreciated parts of the conversation was about CPUs and PCs. Su pushed back on the idea that GPUs would “take over the world” and leave CPUs structurally disadvantaged.

Instead, she argued that AI is actually reviving CPU demand:

Over the last 3 months, what we’ve seen is really a significant uptick in CPU demand… as AI moves to more inferencing and there’s more work being done in things like agent workloads… they’re spawning more general-purpose CPU needs.

Source: Lisa Su, UBS Conference.

Her framing is simple but powerful.

Basically, if you have “1,000 agents or 1,000 virtual employees,” they need somewhere to run, operate on data, and do work, and that’s general-purpose CPU territory.

AI isn’t just a GPU story for AMD. It touches every part of AMD’s portfolio, accelerators, CPUs, and even higher-end PCs.

Risks: Supply, Power, Competition

None of this is risk-free.

Su highlighted issues which we have already flagged.

Limitations of memory, CoWoS packaging, and increasingly, power availability in data center regions.

However, the CEO was reasonably confident AMD can secure what it needs, pointing to deep partnerships with TSMC, memory vendors and packagers, and noting that governments are moving to accelerate power build-outs.

Final Thoughts

This UBS appearance was, in many ways, a live confirmation of the thesis I laid out in my last AMD article.

AMD has deliberately repositioned itself around high-performance computing and AI.

In doing so, it has built a unique, chiplet-driven portfolio that spans CPUs, GPUs, FPGAs and semi-custom, while moving up the stack into rack-scale systems with Helios and ZT.

I still see a realistic path to my $775 base case with EPS approaching $15 and a 25x multiple, and a credible bull scenario closer to $1,000 per share if AMD executes on its share ambitions in a $1 trillion data center market.

While some companies in AI are overpromising and thrashing, AMD is doing something far more valuable: it is quietly executing.

data.title: Alamo Group: Appealing Territory
data.description:
In the summer, I concluded that shares of specialized equipment producer Alamo Group Inc. (ALG) were running two tracks. Following record results in 2023, the business has seen a normalization as well as divergent performance between both its divisions.

This came amidst a resilient performance of the industrial equipment business, offset by softer vegetation management sales, which combined had a negative impact on the business. While net debt was eliminated, a share price run higher made that valuations moved up to 22 times earnings.

Specialized Equipment

Alamo Group was founded in 1969 and over time has grown to expand into a global special equipment manufacturer, which has grown to operate 27 manufacturing locations, now employing nearly 4,000 workers.

The business went public in 1993 and operates 40 brands across two main divisions: industrial equipment and vegetation management. Typical applications include forestry, biomass production, land maintenance, landscaping, mowing, street sweeping, sewer cleaning, highway cleaning, snow & ice removal, and others.

The company has quite a diverse customer mix from its $1.6 billion revenue base. Government-related clients make up 40-50% of sales, with industrial/contractor clients making up 35-40% of sales and agriculture clients representing between 15-20% of sales. Note that sales are split pretty evenly between the two divisions, although industrial equipment is relatively more profitable than vegetation management and over time has become more important, certainly in recent times.

Over the past decade, Alamo has essentially doubled sales to $1.7 billion in 2023, driven by organic growth, a $300 million deal for Morbark, as well as a boom in operations after the pandemic. This is a solid achievement, in between a flattish share count and modest margin gains delivered upon.

Boom Times Reverse

After a boom in 2023, the company saw 2024 sales down 4% to $1.63 billion, but there is a real story behind this. After all, industrial equipment sales rose by 19% to $843 million, with vegetation management sales down 20% to $785 million.

The mix effects meant that operating margins were down 160 basis points to 10.1% of sales, with GAAP earnings down about $1.70 per share to $9.63 per share, as adjusted earnings were reported at $10.12 per share. Note that this net debt load was completely eliminated, and while a backlog of $668 million looked to be a substantial number, it was down from a $900 million number in 2023.

The declining backlog weighed on the business, with first-quarter sales down 8% to $391 million, driven by continued weakness in vegetation. Despite this observation, shares had risen to $227 over the summer.

With earnings power seen around $10 per share, a 22-23 times earnings multiple was rather demanding, with the mix of a softer operating performance and higher share price being rather unattractive.

A Big Reversal

In the near half a year since I last covered the business, shares of Alamo Group have gradually come down to $165 per share, with shares down a quarter in a rather short period of time.

The business has gotten a small impetus as it announced completion of the purchase of Ring-O-Matic, a provider of industrial vacuum excavation equipment, in a transaction seen as adding sales by $25 million per annum.

Over the summer, the situation stabilized, as second-quarter sales were reported up minimally on the year before, after a softer start to the year. The backlog was stable at $687 million, having risen minimally from year-end 2024 levels, as earnings were up minimally compared to the year before and a strong balance sheet was maintained.

In November, third-quarter sales were reported up nearly 5% on the year before to $420 million, with revenues to date down minimally now. Growth was aided by lower percentage sales declines at the vegetation management business. Year to date, earnings are pretty flat at $7.31 per share, making a $10 per share run rate realistic, as third-quarter earnings were down minimally despite a return of topline sales growth.

So far so good, as the order intake seems really volatile, with the sum of the backlog of both segments totaling $618 million now, these levels having come under further pressure so far this year.

And Now?

At this point in time, Alamo Group Inc. has reported a net cash position again, to the tune of $35 million, after the acquisition of Ring-O-Matic seems to have taken place at a near $18 million purchase price. By now, the 12.1 million shares command much lower valuations at $162 per share, as earnings power of $10 and change means that earnings multiples essentially have fallen from 22 times to 16 times earnings here.

While the revenue growth looks solid, it comes in part as Ring-O-Matic contributes about 1.5% to sales growth, as pricing is up as well, driven in part by tariffs on top of regular inflationary trends.

Right now, the situation looks a lot more compelling, as a 4.5% earnings yield has increased to a 6% earnings yield amidst largely similar conditions, at least in my view, compared to the summer. Of course, the company faces some challenges with farmers being hit by tariffs and inflationary pressures, as the same applies to other (governmental) customers as well.

In either case, the significant valuation multiple compression makes that appeal rapidly on the increase in my view. Trading at 16 times earnings, with an earnings yield well in the 6s, and with a modest net cash balance maintained, the valuation is very much de-risked here. Moreover, there is real potential as the vegetation business continues to show a soft performance. However, a real turnaround in this business reveals real potential here.

Given all this, I am gradually turning more upbeat on shares of Alamo here. While shares are not a high-flier, a decent risk-reward position here makes me turn more upbeat here. I do not expect a major turnaround anytime soon, but over time, interesting entry points should provide for a solid return expectation.

data.title: KE Holdings: Tough To Turn Bullish If Macro Continues To Stay Weak
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about KE Holdings (BEKE) previously with a downgrade to a hold rating as the momentum was fading, and the near-term story wasn’t attractive anymore. My read on BEKE hasn’t changed much since my last update. I still keep a hold rating given the property market is still in a downturn. With transaction volumes deteriorating, I believe the near-term story remains challenged.

Home rental and renovation

In September, when I wrote about BEKE’s Q2 earnings update, the area that struck me as positive was that home renovation and rental services were adding revenue but weighing on profitability. This doesn’t seem to be the case anymore; the impact of scaling up the revenue base is showing up in the numbers. To be exact, both home renovation and home rental are now at profitable levels (before headquarters allocations). The more granular details also reinforce my point.

For reference, the home rental contribution margin expanded 430 bps y/y to 8.7%, primarily driven by the mix shift toward the asset-light Carefree Rent model and operating leverage. The Carefree Rent model is BEKE's decentralized rental property management service. Instead of acting as a traditional landlord (which is extremely asset-heavy), BEKE manages properties on behalf of owners (handling things like finding tenants and maintaining the property). The revenue model changes too. Instead of booking the gross rental income as revenue and owner payments as costs, BEKE now records the service fee as net revenue. Mathematically, this directly drives up margin as the profit dollars (numerator) remain stable or grow, and the reported revenue (denominator) is lower (gross to net).

The average units managed per property manager also grew from ~90 to >130, which suggests the platform is scaling more efficiently. This is close to a ~50% improvement in productivity, which is not something that fell from the sky or BEKE adding a pair of hands to the manager. There are structural reasons to believe this productivity is durable. What they did was role specialization (i.e., separating leasing tasks from maintenance and service operations), and they also integrated AI to automate finding tenants and figuring out the right pricing. As an investor, what we are seeing is that a single manager is now able to handle a much larger portfolio without a linear increase in time spent. Consequently, this means that fixed labor costs are now spread over a wider base of properties, which means higher operating leverage.

The home renovation segment also has a similar efficiency story, with contribution margins at 32%. I am also of the view that this is sustainable because it is driven by a better supply chain and not just a price increase or anything one-off. What BEKE did was to expand centralized procurement to 13 categories (more bargaining power), force standardization (making it easier to buy in bulk and easier inventory management), and drive down material costs. All of these have led to some cases reducing procurement prices for specific SKUs by 20%. The integration of Building Information Modeling (BIM) and AI has also helped in automating design proposals, which (like what we have seen in home rental) each employee can now handle much more workload without a linear increase in time spent. In other words, more sales can flow through the pipeline (shorter sales cycles), and there is little associated increase in labor cost, ultimately translating to higher margin contribution.

The procurement unit price of some products has decreased by over 20%. The effectiveness of the cost optimization has been reflected in the financial reports, with the proportion of material-related costs as a percentage of revenue in Q3 decreasing by about 1 percentage point compared to last year average. Q3 2025 earnings call

The case for being neutral

However, I still can’t turn bullish on the near term because the macro situation is dragging down the business much faster than Home Rental/Renovation is improving. Recent data show that home prices are continuing to fall. In October 2025, new home prices fell 0.5% month-on-month, marking the steepest monthly decline in a year, and were down 2.2% y/y. Resale values, across cities of all tiers, are also falling, which I believe still reflects poor buyer confidence and declining demand. The forward-looking estimates, per S&P Global Ratings, also point to a poor demand outlook. They estimated nationwide primary property sales to fall 8% in 2025 and drop another 6–7% in 2026. Even if one were to think that China can continue its “investment” strategy to drive a recovery, the data suggests otherwise. The fact is investment into property is also drying up. Fixed-asset investment tied to real estate is also contracting sharply.

BEKE’s internal data also paint a negative outlook. Take the new home segment data, for example. Q3 2025 saw new home GTV fall 13.7% y/y, and net revenue decline 14.1%. But the bigger problem is the shift in consumer preference. Management noted that customers are leaning toward existing homes because pricing is more attractive in that channel. Since we are at this point, this dynamic of having more existing home sales also results in a negative mix shift to the top line, as new home transactions have higher take rates vs. existing home sales (based on Q3 2025 data).

Anyway, the more I read up about the situation, the more I am convinced China is not going to get out of this slump anytime soon. Despite the government’s efforts to cut borrowing costs, ease buyer restrictions, and offer subsidies, there are simply no signs of market stabilization. For BEKE, whose business relies almost entirely on real estate transactions and buyer confidence, this is possibly the worst-case scenario (outside of a subprime crisis) for the business. The bottom line for me here is that until China’s property market finds a bottom and buyer sentiment recovers meaningfully, the stock sentiment is not going to improve, and that will weigh on valuations.

Valuation

I am quite surprised BEKE’s multiples saw a positive re-rating over the past few months, with it now at 21x forward PE. I think the market may have put too much weight on the new initiatives, which, to their merit, are doing really well and are not focusing on the core issue. The main concern here is BEKE is not able to grow without a macro recovery. There are simply no signs that suggest we are near an inflection point. All the hard data points to poor demand, and even if there is demand, it comes with a negative mix (new homes to existing homes). Therefore, I am not keen to think that multiples will go higher from here. I still hold my view that the stock will remain range-bound +/- at the 20x range until there is a catalyst to drive a broad macro property demand recovery.

Conclusion

I reiterate a hold rating for BEKE. The new businesses are scaling cleanly, but the macro headwinds are simply too deep to ignore. Home prices are still falling, transaction volumes are weakening, and the mix shift toward existing home sales adds pressure to margins. Until the property market shows real signs of stabilizing, the stock is unlikely to rerate meaningfully.

data.title: Hayward Holdings Is Doing Great, But This Doesn't Mean To Dip Into Its Shares
data.description:
Even though I don't do it nearly as much as I would like to, I absolutely love swimming. Growing up in Rittman, Ohio, my family had a small above-ground pool that we would indulge in on hot summer days. Today, the swimming pool is still above ground, but it's larger. Regardless, it is a great way to spend some time if you want to relax and perhaps get in some exercise. Because of how much care I have for this activity, it brings me great pleasure when I can analyze companies dedicated to this space. Currently, there are very few such firms. But one of them is none other than Hayward Holdings, Inc. (HAYW), a $3.31 billion business that produces and sells pool equipment and related automation systems.

Even though I like this space and the companies in it, I need to be objective when I write about them. That's why, in early August of this year, I called Hayward Holdings a better "Hold" candidate than a "Buy" candidate. At that time, I noticed that financial performance was improving. Revenue, profits, and cash flows were all moving higher. But at the end of the day, I believed that the stock was more fairly valued than undervalued. Since then, the market has disagreed with me some, pushing the stock up 8.8% at a time when the S&P 500 is up 5.9%. But still, I stand by my earlier assessment, with the company looking pretty convincingly fairly valued.

Taking A Dip With Hayward Holdings

Operationally speaking, Hayward Holdings is a fascinating business. About 90% of the firm's revenue comes from the residential pool market. The remaining 10% is split pretty evenly between the commercial pool and flow control categories. The products that it sells include automation and sanitation products, pumps, heaters, filters, lighting and water features, and a wide array of other offerings. But the first four that I listed here are the largest, accounting for a combined 73% of revenue. Unlike what you might expect, Hayward Holdings is also a global business. Admittedly, about 78% of its revenue comes from the United States. But it also gets large chunks of revenue from Europe, Canada, and other miscellaneous international markets.

At face value, the swimming pool industry may not seem all that exciting. However, management is working hard to create meaningful value for shareholders. During the most recent quarter, which would be the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, management showed the fruits of their laborers. Revenue during that time amounted to $244.3 million. That was up 7.3% over the $227.6 million the company reported for last year. This increase in revenue was driven largely by higher prices that added 5.4% to the top line for the business. Some of this was certainly because of tariffs, as management detailed. Inflationary pressures outside of those caused by tariffs also played a role. Foreign currency fluctuations also contributed 0.5% to the sales increase. And on top of that, volumes grew by 1.5%. The pricing increases are always great to see. And that's because, to the extent that they exceed the overall costs of revenue for the company, they are all profit for the business. But I think it would be a disservice not to laud the company for the volume improvement as well, especially in such a difficult market.

By management's own admission, the higher volume was attributable to favorable timing when it came to orders for the 2025 season. That's why, for the first nine months of 2025 instead, volume contributed only 0.5% to the top line for the company instead of the higher 1.5% that we saw in the third quarter on its own. But this does not mean that management is not making progress in some really key areas. Today, the firm has over 550 current and pending patents globally. It continues to invest in certain growth opportunities on the innovation front. In fact, the company has acknowledged that it is accelerating plans to grow in the commercial pool and flow control markets.

When it comes to the flow control space specifically, the company said in its latest earnings call that it achieved double-digit growth when it came to omni-control offerings. This specifically refers to the OmniX automation platform. This is a smart pool automation system that grants owners smart and remote control over their existing pool equipment. This operates as a sort of modular system where you buy specific components, like those for pumps, that are enabled to be used on this platform. You set up these devices, and they communicate with one another, as well as with your home network or smartphone, to give you more control and flexibility over controlling, scheduling, and monitoring your pool equipment. One example here of this technology in action would be water temperature monitoring. As time goes on, we should expect this technology and other ones like it to create even more upside for the business.

The increase in revenue that management achieved brought with it higher profits and cash flows as well. Net profits popped from $16.5 million to $24 million year over year. Operating cash flow grew from $66 million to $94.6 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a rise from $32.5 million to $46.7 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business expanded from $51.1 million to $59.1 million. In the chart above, you can also see financial results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time last year. Revenue, profits, and cash flows all grew year over year without exception.

Management certainly seems optimistic about the near-term outlook. They anticipate revenue this year of between $1.095 billion and $1.11 billion. This would be up from the $1.05 billion reported for 2024. And it also represents an increase over the previous guidance of between $1.07 billion and $1.10 billion. Management also anticipates EBITDA this year of between $292 million and $297 million. Previous guidance called for this to be between $280 million and $290 million, while last year we got a reading of $277.4 million. Based on the current trajectory of other profitability metrics, it's likely that net profits will be somewhere around $154.6 million, while adjusted operating cash flow should come out somewhere around $231.2 million.

Armed with these estimates and also with the historical results that the company achieved for the 2024 fiscal year, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently valued. These trading multiples aren't bad. But I wouldn't exactly call them great either. It is worth noting, however, that shares are a bit cheaper than most other similar terms. In that same chart, I stacked Hayward Holdings up against three other pool-oriented enterprises. On a price-to-operating-cash-flow basis, it was the cheapest of the group. But when it came to the EV-to-EBITDA approach, one of the three firms ended up being cheaper than it is. This shows that while the stock might be mostly fairly valued on an absolute basis, it is perhaps a bit cheap compared to similar firms.

Takeaway

The way I view things right now, Hayward Holdings is a solid business that's doing a fine job. This global enterprise, while small, is working on some innovative technologies aimed at creating more growth for shareholders moving forward. The stock is perhaps a bit cheap compared to other similar enterprises, but it is still in the fair value range, as I see it. I also worry about broader economic uncertainty. Although it might be resistant to any sort of decline, mainly because those who own pools need equipment even during a recession, I believe that a worsening in the economic climate will create some challenges in the near term. This is not to imply that I expect financial performance to worsen. It's likely that growth might slow down some. When you add that on top of the valuation component, I believe that maintaining it as a "Hold" candidate is the right choice.

data.title: Apple: Inventory Does Not Lie
data.description:
AAPL Q4 Inventory In Focus

I last covered Apple Inc. (AAPL) on Oct 24 with an article titled “Apple FQ4 Earnings Preview: $4 Trillion Market Capitalization Ahead.” As stated in the title, that article served a preview for the company’s earnings report (ER), released on Oct 30, for its 4th fiscal quarter. The article focused on its double-compounding mechanism – the combined effect of robust earnings growth and large scale repurchases - to accelerate its market cap appreciation. Indeed, the company’s MC has quickly pushed above $4T after the ER, as seen in the next chart.

Besides the stock price changes, its FQ4 ER also provided a few other materials developments worthy of a follow-up. As one of the most followed stocks on the Seeking Alpha site, most aspects of its ER have been covered by other writers already by this point. As such, here I will concentrate on an aspect that has been largely overlooked in other earnings reviews: its inventory. As a background, I have been following these insights from Peter Lynch on the use of inventory data over the years and have fortunately enjoyed good results:

To start, unlike many other financial data that are more open to interpretation, inventory is one of the less ambiguous financial data. Lynch also explained why inventory levels can be a telltale sign of business cycles. Especially for cyclical businesses, inventory buildup is a warning sign, which indicates the company (or sector) might be overproducing while the demand is already softening. Conversely, depleting inventory could be an early sign of a recovery.

In the case of AAPL, its FQ4 financials certainly point to continued growth in my view. More specifically, the screenshot below shows AAPL's Total Revenues and inventory reported for FQ4. As seen, its sales keep experiencing a healthy year-over-year (YoY) increase, growing by 7.86% from $94,930 million in September 2024 to $102,466 million in FQ4 2025. To make the good news even better, the company also reported a significant reduction in its Inventory, which dropped by 21.5% over the same period. The Inventory value decreased from $7,286 million in September 2024 to $5,718 million in FQ4 2025.

Next, I will argue why this sharp decline suggests improved operational efficiency and stronger demand leading to quicker inventory turnover, both of which are likely to further accelerate as the company’s profit composition evolves.

AAPL Inventory Can Become Even Lighter And More Efficient

Thanks to the company’s continued investments in its global supply chain and product management cycles, the company has been able to support more revenue in recent years with less inventory on hand, as you can see from the chart above. Expanding our horizon a bit, between July 2023 and September 2025, its sales grew by about 12.5% from $81,797 million to $102,466 million, while inventory dropped by more than 22% from $7,351 million to $5,718.

Further widening our timeframe, the next chart shows AAPL’s DOI (days of outstanding inventory) data in the past 5 years since 2021. As seen, its DOI has fluctuated within a relatively tight range during this period, with a brief peak of around 15 days. Most of the time, the DOI has stayed between a range of 9 days to 12 days only, with an average of 10.5 days. The current reading as of FQ4 is 9.815 days and is below the historical average.

Looking ahead, I see large odds for AAPL’s business model to further improve and use of inventory to be become even more efficient. Among the many reasons behind this statement, two stand out. First, I expect the company will continue to refine its manufacturing logistics globally following its latest commitments. The company has announced substantial capital investment plans both in the U.S. (total $600 billion) and overseas (with its “China Plus One” strategy in India as a notable example) to strengthen its supply chain resilience.

Second, I expect the company to keep leveraging the rapidly growing and zero-inventory Services segment going forward. To better contextualize things, the chart below illustrates the significant growth of Apple's services revenue over the past decade. As seen, the revenue from this segment grew substantially from $16.8 billion in 2014 to $109.2 billion as of TTM FQ 2025. This massive increase was fueled by some of the most beloved and popular services among its fans, including Apple Pay, Apple Music, Apple TV, Apple Arcade, etc. As of FY 2025, services accounted for 26% of Apple's total revenue and contributed to an impressive 52% of the company's overall revenue growth.

Other Risks And Final Thoughts

In terms of downside risks, the valuation is certainly not cheap. After the price really since the ER release, the stock now trades at a FWD P/E ratio of around 33.5x, as you can see from the following chart. This is not only elevated in absolute terms, but is also toward the upper end of the spectrum by the Mag 7 standard.

In terms of business fundamental risks, the company faces the same set of risks generic to other mega-cap technology stocks such as competition, rapid products' evolution, etc. Here I will focus more on a risk that is directly related to my argument in this article. With AAPL’s services revenue, revenue related to games represents the largest share (at around 44%). This part of the revenue now faces a key uncertainty with the ongoing Apple vs. Epic Games case. Earlier in the year, Apple has issued warnings that the ruling could cause negative impacts on the order of “hundreds of millions to billions dollars.” I don’t think it’s an exaggeration to consider the outcome of this case a landmark for Apple's future business model and profitability of its App Store.

To wrap up, this revisit to AAPL stock is motivated by its FQ4 ER released since my last writing. In particular, I believe the inventory data is an important – yet overlooked - bullish signal. AAPL’s successful strategy of increasing sales while reducing physical stock creates more room to improve cash conversion cycles and further optimize capital allocation. Looking ahead, I expect the potency of its strategy to increase in the years ahead as the company further streamlines its logistic network and expands its service revenue.

data.title: Wise: Focus On The Long-Term Fundamentals, Ignore Near-Term Noise
data.description:
Investment action

I had a buy rating for Wise Plc (OTCPK:WPLCF) previously, as I believed the business had a clear growth runway, and it has a solid edge to continue capturing demand. My stance on WPLCF is still a buy rating. The Platform business is scaling nicely as expected; WPLCF is also widening its moat, and customer growth remains strong.

Wise Platform Has Hit a Critical Inflection Point

Six months ago, my view on the Platform segment was that it could eventually represent a sizeable portion of the business. Fast forward to today, I still believe my view is right, and the most recent data point did show that Platform now makes up a little above 5% of total cross-border volume. This may look non-significant, but I assure you it is. The focus here is the pace of growth, which implies that the segment is extremely fast at ~80% y/y growth (you can back into this math by using last year's share of total cross-border volume and the increase in total volume growth).

I believe this strong growth rate also tells us that the banks are effectively acknowledging that WPLCF cross-border infrastructure is a better solution than their in-house or competing offerings. The halo effect of winning more major partners also reinforces the network effect, in that by landing one major institution, it draws interest from others, and that makes each new partnership a catalyst for the next.

Also, like I said before, since these integrations sit on top of WPLCF's existing rails and require limited incremental sales effort once they go live, the incremental volume comes with very little incremental cost. And by that definition, if Platform moves to management’s 10% medium-term target, the mix shift should support margin expansion.

Widening the Moat

While only six months have passed since my initiation, WPLCF has managed to widen its moat. Specifically, its cross-border rails infrastructure moat. To give a better sense of things, as of the latest number, 74% of transactions settle in under 20 seconds, which is up from 63% a year ago. For context, in cross-border payments, to achieve that level of speed, one must bypass the traditional network and be connected directly into domestic payment systems. WPLCF now has eight of these direct connections, with Brazil’s Pix already live and Japan’s Zengin coming online next.

This advantage also shows up in the unit economics. If we look at the latest numbers, WPLCF has managed to cut its average cross-border take rate by 10 bps y/y to 0.52%, and yet the underlying gross profit margin still expanded to 76.2%. My view is very simple here. Get the cost down and drive in volume so much that the operating leverage (cost per transaction) outweighs the cut in take rate. And this is playing out as expected; the lower take rate has resulted in more volume (up 24% to GBP 84.9 billion in 1H26). As WPLCF keeps this up, the gap between it and sub-scale peers continues to widen, and at some point, it would almost be impossible to replicate this moat. If the volume data is not enough, the customer growth data also reinforces the same story. WPLCF added 3.5 million new active customers in 1H26, up 14% y/y, now with a total active customer count of 13.4 million.

Near-Term Noise

The market clearly doesn’t share my optimism since the share price is down a bunch since the earnings and my initiation. From what I am seeing, it seems to be the WPLCF margin compression aspect that is impacting sentiment. Despite all the growth and whatnot, underlying PBT margins declined sharply to 16.3% from 22.2% last year. This has probably spooked some investors and caused the sell-down. However, I don’t think this is a sign of weakening fundamentals. The unit economics have improved as mentioned above; the decline is due to WPLCF reinvesting ahead of demand. It makes all the sense in the world for WPLCF to invest, given the growth and scale advantage. What WPLCF specifically did was to hire more people (>1,000 people in 1H, which is already above the full-year target) to further improve the infrastructure (e.g., compliance, product, etc.).

You can already see the payoff in the revenue mix. Non-cross-border income now accounts for 41% of total underlying revenue, showing that WPLCF is successfully moving customers from remittance usage (which is occasional and not predictable) to broader international banking activity (the more use cases, the more volume WPLCF can tap into). To put some numbers in context, card revenue grew 28% y/y to £132 million, and customer balances grew 37% to >GBP25 billion. These metrics clearly point to deeper engagement and a higher lifetime value per customer, which supports the decision to invest more upfront in people and product.

I must also point out that the margin noise is purely optical. WPLCF absorbed GBP35 million of one-off expenses tied to its planned US dual listing. If we adjust for these costs, the underlying PBT margin would have been 17.8% in 1H26, which is well within the long-term target range.

Valuation

I am not too worried about the noise that the market is focusing on. The fundamentals remain strong, and the decision to invest ahead makes a lot of sense. The management team is clearly playing the long-term game rather than focusing on the quarterly fluctuations. I’d place my bet on a team like this. As such, I am not changing my model assumptions. I still model high-teen growth forward and margins to expand as revenue scales. The only thing I changed is to upgrade the NTM PE to 29x (the average over the past 3 years). I think WPLCF deserves an average given these improved fundamentals.

Risk

The risk I see is the heavy investment cycle may compress margins longer than expected. I get that WPLCF is betting heavily on future growth. But if top-line growth disappoints, my worry is the market will punish the stock further. Another risk is stablecoin adoption. While management dismisses this as an immediate threat, rapid stablecoin adoption could theoretically bypass fiat rails entirely, and that challenges WPLCF's core value proposition in the long run.

Conclusion

I stay buy-rated for WPLCF. The margin compression problem is temporary, while the fundamentals continue to get better (platform expansion, stronger unit economics, and deeper customer relationships). Management is investing ahead of demand, and the early payoff is already visible in mix and scale. I believe high-teen growth is realistic, and margins should expand as volumes grow.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ESPO: A Focused Global Play On The Gaming And ESport Market
data.description:
Introduction to The VanEck Video Gaming and eSports ETF

VanEck, a New York-based investment management firm that is noted for its niche ETFs, currently offers investors the opportunity to participate in the broad Video Gaming and eSports universe in the shape of a $400 million-sized product. The VanEck Video Gaming and eSports ETF (ESPO) has been around since October 2018, and currently has an expense ratio of 0.56%. ESPO, which currently consists of 25 global stocks, also makes distributions once a year, with the yield working to 0.33%

What Does ESPO Do?

ESPO is not an actively managed ETF; rather it aims to replicate an index called the MVIS Global Video Gaming and eSports Index [MGVGEI], which is designed to cover some of the largest and most liquid stocks in the global video gaming and eSports (a subcategory in sports that basically deals with competitive organized video gaming) industries.

To make it to this index, global stocks are required to generate at least half their revenue from the activities linked to video gaming (this could include not just game development, but also software and hardware related to gaming, streaming services) and eSports events. Besides this revenue requirement, stocks are also required to clear certain liquidity (Average 3-month daily trading volumes of $1 million, and 250 thousand shares traded per month over a 6-month duration) and market-cap screeners (minimum of $150 million). Currently, MGVGEI focuses on 25 stocks where the maximum weight of each stock is capped at 8% of the portfolio, and is carried out 4 times in a year.

]jh≠./x;,Main Characteristics of ESPO’s Portfolio

The first thing to be said is that this is a very narrow portfolio of only 25 stocks, and an 8% capping weight (as opposed to say 5%) means the top 10 stocks will likely take up a huge chunk of the portfolio (they currently account for 62% of the total portfolio), making this very top-heavy.

Even though we have an 8% cap, note that ESPO’s tracking index is still primarily weighted on the basis of these stocks’ individual market-caps, so even though one gets access to small-caps (the additional liquidity and volume screeners will largely eradicate micro-caps) tilt towards giant and large-caps which make up 58% of the portfolio in aggregate. ESPO’s average market-cap of $27B also puts it in the large-cap territory.

This is supposed to be a global portfolio and one gets access to stocks from 9 different countries, with stocks from two countries in particular- Japan and US dominating with weights of 28% each.

As far as traditional sector classification is concerned, the bulk of the holdings come from the communication sector, with rest comprising of discretionary and tech sector stocks. What’s common between all three are they are high-beta sectors with strong growth characteristics.

Speaking of growth (stocks with above average sales and earnings growth, strong momentum, relatively pricey valuations, etc.) and other styles, the former cohort does enjoy a useful stake accounting for 42% of the portfolio, but at the end of the day, hybrid stocks still dominate with a 55% weight (stocks with both growth and value characteristics).

Risks Associated With ESPO

One could argue that ESPO doesn’t do a great job of fully replicating its benchmark index. We say this because the tracking errors shown in the image below (across different durations) are roughly twice as much as what other ETFs experience, and for a narrow portfolio of 25-27 stocks that isn’t good enough.

Elevated tracking errors aren’t the only concern; even the variability of ESPO’s returns tend to be quite widely divergent from its average returns and that is captured by above-average standard deviation and annualized volatility readings. This makes ESPO quite an unideal product to utilize as a core holding in one’s portfolio.

ESPO is also not particularly cost-efficient, priced at an net expense ratio of 0.56% that is more than the median expense ratio of ETFs. Also take note of ESPO’s wide differential between its bid and its ask (in percentage terms it is almost twice as much as what other ETFs experience). Products with such wide bid-asks tend to cause slippage risks (the risk of entering and exiting positions at prices that are quite different from what was initially budgeted for).

Investors should also consider that a good chunk of this portfolio (close to two-thirds) remains vulnerable to foreign currency depreciation versus the USD. ESPO may be a USD denominated product, but only 35% of its holdings are denominated in USD terms with the rest being denominated in global currencies particularly the JPY and the HKD. Thus, even if those foreign stocks do well in their local currencies, if those currencies end up depreciating against the dollar during the same reporting period, investors of ESPO could end up taking a hit on their net returns.

Prima facie, since ESPO is supposed to cover global stocks, it may give investors the false illusion of being a solid diversifier away from the US markets. But that isn’t necessarily true, and it tends to move in the same direction as the benchmark equity index.

This can be gleaned from a positive and moderately strong correlation of 0.65x, and a relatively high beta co-efficient of 0.75x (for every $1 movement in the S&P500, ESPO typically follows by $0.75.

ESPO’s valuations too are hardly compelling, trading roughly on par with the S&P500 on a price-to-earnings basis, and trading at a steeper premium of 19% on a price-to-cashflow basis.

The relatively pricier cash flow multiple also implies that ESPO’s holdings don’t have a meaningful enough free cash flow [FCF)) base that is widely seen as a mark of quality. One could argue that the lack of meaningful implied FCF is also because these gaming and eSports businesses are devoting a lot of resources to R&D [Research & Development] which often includes a lot of speculative bets that tend to result in sub-par outcomes or project that are eventually shelved.

Who Is ESPO ETF For?

We noted earlier how ESPO’s P/E valuations don’t look meaningfully cheap to the S&P500, but do also consider that for that roughly similar P/E multiple, ESPO’s holdings are staring at a much more attractive runway of earnings growth, which is also much better than the Nasdaq

Put another way, as we implied earlier (hybrid stocks), ESPO has the look of a product that would appeal to GARP (Growth-at-a-reasonable-price) type investors.

Contrary to popular belief, video gaming is also no longer a niche terrain that only appeals to the youngsters of this world. Infact the addressable market is now becoming a lot broader with even a wider proportion of Millennials and Boomers taking to it.

With E-sports the market is still relatively nascent, and the growth potential is even more enormous with the global market poised to almost triple by the end of this decade.

Basically, by getting onboard with ESPO you are getting access to a rapidly-expanding and increasingly popular entertainment terrain that is spreading its tentacles across vast swathes of the global population as time progresses.

Then, if you’re someone who wants to get hold of industries where AI penetration is progressing at a rapid pace, and leading to strong efficiencies, this product works as a great theme. AI is particularly deeply entrenched in game development (see image below), but you’re also seeing that in pockets such as game execution, and monetization as well.

What ETF Alternatives Are There Besides ESPO?

ESPO is by far the largest ETF in the video-gaming and e-sports terrain, but if investors are not happy with ESPO’s qualities and want some lesser-known alternatives, they may also consider the Global X Video Games & Esports ETF (HERO) or the NERD Video Games ETF (NERD) which also happen to be more cost-efficient and offer far superior yields than our product in focus.

HERO, which got listed a year after ESPO, has so far amassed AUM of $120M, and passively tracks an index called the Solactive Video Games & Esports Index. It offers the widest reach (48 stocks, almost double of ESPO’s coverage), with some useful micro-cap exposure, and an average market-cap that is closer to mid-cap territory. HERO is also the only ETF of the three which pays dividends twice a year. However, what we find very concerning with HERO is the inordinately wide bid-asks associated with it, despite having better AUM and daily liquidity than NERD

NERD stands out because it is not passively managed like the other two but has an active manager picking stocks, with a greater tilt towards Japanese stocks and the communication services sector that the other two portfolios. Despite being actively managed, it is also the most stable portfolio of the three with the lowest annual turnover ratio. Top-heaviness seems to be an issue here as well, despite covering a much wider pool of stocks than our product in focus. However, it also offers a yield that is 4x better than ESPO.

Having said all this, investors may still want to note that when it comes to delivering the goods (so far), ESPO has comfortably outperformed the other two portfolios since NERD (the youngest product of the three) got listed.

Summary

ESPO is a focused, growth-oriented, global ETF for investors who want to gain access to a market category (Video game and e-sports) that is seeing tremendous traction across the world. However, ESPO may not offer the most cost-efficient exposure to this space, yields an unremarkable figure, and like most alternatives in this space, is also likely to be susceptible to slippage risk.

This article answers these three main questions about ESPO:

  1. What are the main characteristics of the ESPO portfolio?
  2. What are the major risks associated with ESPO, and what type of investor should use this product?
  3. How does ESPO compare to other ETFs in the gaming and eSport space?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.
